text: The surgeon identifies an indirect inguinal hernia in a female patient, visible as a bulge in the groin area. An ultrasound confirmed the presence of the hernia. Upon entry, a lipoma within the canal is reduced. The surgeon proceeds with medial dissection, aiming to establish a clear view of the myopectineal orifice, crossing the midline during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a retropubic dissection between Cooper's ligament and the bladder, using the obturator foramen as a marker. Fat is reduced from the obturator area. The round ligament is transected to allow the mesh to lay flat, followed by lateral dissection.
The surgeon observes retroperitoneal fat in the surgical field and prepares to place the mesh.

The surgeon places the mesh over the hernia site, ensuring good circumferential coverage. The mesh is adjusted into position, followed by flap closure using a 3-0 V-lock suture. The mesh is selectively fixated, as the hernia is assessed to be low risk.

During flap closure, the surgeon identifies a small fenestration and proceeds to close it using a six-inch suture. Another small hole is visible to the right, but the surgeon decides it is not necessary to close.
